metadata.title: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Classical physics says a hot oven should blast you with infinite ultraviolet light. Open one and it plainly doesn't. Fixing that contradiction started quantum mechanics.

card.copy: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Classical physics says a hot oven should blast you with infinite ultraviolet light. Open one and it plainly doesn't. Fixing that cont

beats.narration_text: Heat a metal box to 3000 Kelvin. The walls radiate and re-absorb light continuously. Classical physics predicts exactly how much energy each frequency mode should carry — and the answer blows up.

The Rayleigh-Jeans law: each wave mode gets k-B times T of energy on average, no matter how short its wavelength. More modes fit at shorter wavelengths — infinitely many. Multiply them out and you get infinite total energy. This is wrong.

card.copy: Classical law: every wave mode gets the same energy. Shorter wavelengths: more modes. Total energy: infinite. But real ovens don't explode. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Rayleigh-Jeans law: each wave mode gets k-B times T of energy on average, no matter how short its wavelength. More modes fit at shorter wavelengths — infini

beats.narration_text: The classical curve climbs steeply at short wavelengths — more and more energy poured into higher and higher frequencies. This is the ultraviolet catastrophe.

Planck's fix: light energy comes in discrete packets — quanta — of size h times frequency. A high-frequency mode can only be excited if it gets one whole quantum. If k-B T is less than h-nu, the mode stays empty.
High-frequency modes are starved: thermal energy is not enough to buy even one quantum. They contribute almost nothing. The energy curve bends back down at short wavelengths.
The Planck curve peaks at a wavelength set by the temperature — hotter boxes peak at shorter wavelengths — then falls back to zero. This is the blackbody spectrum, and it matches the data exactly.
The key insight: energy is not continuous. You cannot give a high-frequency oscillator a little energy — it must receive a full quantum h-nu or nothing. Nature bills in whole packets, not fractions.

Illustrative example: for a box at 3000 Kelvin, k-B T is about 0.26 eV. A visible-light mode at 600 nanometers has a quantum h-nu of about 2.1 eV — eight times larger than the thermal budget. Most visible modes stay nearly empty.
Classical physics gives each mode equal energy and gets infinity. Quantum mechanics gates each mode with a minimum — and gets the right answer.

card.copy: Classical: every mode gets equal energy. Quantum: each mode needs one full packet. High frequencies stay empty. The curve bends back down.
